turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Barrett has the opinion of the Court this morning in Case 22-1008, Corner Post versus The Board of Governors of the Federal Reserve.

turns.name: AMY CONEY BARRETT
turns.text: Sorry, this is not one of the cases that you are waiting to hear, so I will try to be concise. This one is about a statute of limitations. For the non-lawyers in the room, statutes of limitations are like clocks on lawsuits. They tell plaintiffs how much time they have to sue. So if a limitations period is six years and a plaintiff files suit in the seventh year, her claim will be barred. The plaintiff in this case is Corner Post, a small truck stop and convenience store in Watford City, North Dakota. It opened for business in 2018 and became frustrated by the cost of accepting debit cards. While convenient for customers, debit cards are costly for merchants. Every time a retailer like Corner Post swipes a debit card, it must pay an interchange fee to the issuing bank. These fees are set by payment networks like Visa and Mastercard and they quickly add up. Corner Post has paid hundreds of thousands of dollars in fees since it opened and those fees of course have largely been passed on to its customers. To contain interchange fees which were uncontrolled for many years, Congress enacted the Durbin Amendment to the Dodd-Frank Act in 2010. This statute tasks the Federal Reserve Board with ensuring that interchange fees are reasonable and proportional to the bank’s costs. In 2011, the Board promulgated Regulation II, which set a maximum interchange fee, and in 2021 Corner Post joined a lawsuit against the Board arguing that Regulation II sets a higher fee than the Durbin Amendment allows. This is where the statute of limitations comes in. Can Corner Post challenge Regulation II or did Corner Post come to court too late? Corner Post’s claim against the Board arises under the Administrative Procedure Act, the APA for short. The APA doesn’t have its own statute of limitations. Instead, a catch-all statute applies. 28 U.S.C. 2401(a) provides that every civil action commenced against the United States shall be barred unless it is filed within six years after the right of action first accrues. That last phrase is the relevant part because it tells us when the clock starts ticking. When does a right of action first accrue? A right of action is the plaintiff’s claim for relief. Corner Post’s claim arises under the APA and a key point here, which everyone accepts, is that the APA does not allow a plaintiff to sue unless she is injured by final agency action. The injury here is paying the interchange fee. So everyone agrees that Corner Post could not have sued the Board until it started paying those fees when it opened in 2018. Corner Post says that that is when the right of action first accrued for purposes of the statute. In other words, that’s when the clock started ticking. The Board by contrast insists that it started ticking in 2011 when Regulation II became final, even though Corner Post could not have sued and did not exist back then. Under the Board’s approach, a plaintiff injured more than six years after the agency action can never challenge it. The Courts of Appeals are divided on this issue. Most hold that the clock starts running as soon as the agency’s action becomes final, even if the plaintiff wasn’t injured or didn’t exist at the time. The Sixth Circuit by contrast has held that the clock starts only after the plaintiff is injured because that is the first point at which the plaintiff can sue. We granted cert to resolve the split. We agree with the Sixth Circuit. Section 2401(a)’s clock starts when the right of action accrues and that is when the plaintiff has a complete and present cause of action on which she could sue. The answer is compelled by the ordinary meaning of accrues when 2401 was enacted in 1948. As we’ve said before, accrues has had a well-settled meaning from the 19th century up until today. A cause of action accrues when a plaintiff can sue to obtain relief. Legal dictionaries in the 1940s made this clear. If injury is a prerequisite for the plaintiff’s suit, her claim does not accrue when the defendant acts but only after the plaintiff is injured. Our precedent has repeatedly recognized this as the standard rule for limitations periods which Congress legislates against. Here are just a few of the things we’ve said. In a case named Gabelli, a right accrues when the plaintiff has a complete and present cause of action. In Bay Area Laundry, a cause of action does not become complete and present for limitations purposes until the plaintiff can file suit and obtain relief. The possibility that a limitations period commences at a time when the plaintiff could not yet file suit is inconsistent with basic limitations principles. And in a case called Greene, while the standard rule can be displaced such that the limitations period begins to run before a plaintiff can file suit, we will not infer such an odd result in the absence of any such indication in the text of the limitations period. So too here. Nothing in 2401(a)’s text gives any indication that it begins before the plaintiff can sue. In fact, the statute uses the standard language, right of action first accrues, which indicates that’s a normal statute of limitations that works in the way such statutes normally do. That means that the statute of limitations for Corner Post began only after it was injured by Regulation II and could sue under the APA. Its suit was timely. That sounds so straightforward that you might wonder how the Board can argue otherwise, so I’ll briefly address three of its arguments. First, it points primarily to other statutes authorizing specific types of suits against agencies and some of these statutes do start the clock once agency action becomes final. For example, the Hobbs Act requires suits against certain agencies to be filed 60 days after the issuance of any regulation or order, but 2401(a) doesn’t use language like that. It starts the clock in the traditional way when the right of action first accrues. Textual differences matter and if anything, the fact that Congress used different language in these statutes highlights the different choice that it made in 2401(a), and even if statutes with different text could overcome the word accrues’ ordinary meaning, the finality focused statutes largely came after 2401(a) was enacted, or they concerned limitations periods for parties challenging adjudications in which agency action and injury occur at the same time. So there really isn’t a good argument that 2401(a) smuggled a special APA rule into the traditional accrual language. Second, the Board seizes on stray dicta in a couple of early 20th century cases, suggesting that the meaning of accrues depends on context. But those cases don’t help the Board here because we have never said that the same word, here accrues, in a single statute, like 2401(a), changes meaning like a chameleon depending on the cause of action at issue. And in the Board’s primary case, Crown Coat, we held that a cause of action accrued for purposes of 2401(a), the very statute of limitations we’re interpreting here, when the plaintiff had the right to bring suit in court. Third, the Board raises policy arguments, arguing that permitting newcomers like Corner Post to challenge regulations more than six years after finality will leave regulations always subject to challenge. Policy arguments of course can never overcome the text, but we observe that regulations are already always subject to challenge. Even the Board agrees that regulated parties can always challenge regulations in enforcement proceedings within six years of injury, no matter when the injury occurs, and to the extent that plaintiffs like Corner Post can also challenge them, Courts can weed out meritless suits, which will often be foreclosed by precedent anyway. At the end of the day maybe it would be a good idea to cut off all administrative law challenges six years after the agency action even if the plaintiff isn’t injured until later, or maybe not. We have said after all that the APA includes a basic presumption of judicial review and we have a deeply rooted tradition that everyone should have her own day in court. Ultimately though this is a choice for Congress, not us. Our job is to give effect to 2401(a) as Congress enacted it and the statute allows Corner Post to sue for six years from the date of its injury. Because the Eighth Circuit held to the contrary, we reverse its judgment and remand the case for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. Justice Kavanaugh has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Jackson has filed a dissenting opinion, in which Justice Sotomayor and Justice Kagan join.
